title: Trauma and the Body's Center: Finding Your Core After Trauma
excerpt: Explore how trauma impacts the body's center and learn science-backed ways to reconnect with your core for lasting healing.
body:
Understanding the Body's Center in Trauma Recovery

When we talk about the body's center, we're referring to more than just the physical core—the abdomen, pelvis, and lower back. It's the seat of our embodied self, where our nervous system anchors, and where emotional and physiological regulation often begin. Trauma, as Bessel van der Kolk famously noted, is stored not only in the mind but deeply within the body (The Body Keeps the Score, 2014). This article unpacks how trauma disrupts your connection to your center and offers evidence-based pathways to reclaim it.

Why the Core Matters: Neuroscience and Somatics

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory highlights the autonomic nervous system's role in safety and connection. The vagus nerve, which is central to this theory, innervates the core organs and muscles that stabilize our posture and regulate breath. When trauma hijacks this system, it creates a state of dysregulation that often manifests as a disconnection from the body’s core sensations (Porges, 2011).

Peter Levine’s Somatic Experiencing emphasizes the importance of tracking bodily sensations—especially in the core region—to renegotiate trauma responses. The 'felt sense' of the body’s center becomes a compass for healing, as it holds the tension, immobilization, or collapse induced by traumatic events (Levine, 1997).

The Impact of Trauma on the Body’s Center

Trauma often triggers survival responses: fight, flight, freeze, or collapse. These responses are deeply physical and frequently localized around the core. For example, many trauma survivors report chronic tightness, pain, or numbness in the abdomen or lower back. Pat Ogden's Sensorimotor Psychotherapy research describes how trauma can create patterns of muscular guarding and physiological shutdown in the core area (Ogden, Minton & Pain, 2006).

Gabor Maté also highlights how early trauma can lead to disconnection from bodily signals, especially hunger, thirst, and internal emotional cues—all processed through the body’s center (Maté, 2010). This disconnect impairs self-regulation and emotional resilience.

Blockquote

"Trauma is not just an event that took place in the past; it is also the imprint left by that experience on mind, brain, and body." – Bessel van der Kolk

Reclaiming Your Core: Somatic Approaches to Healing

Reconnecting with your core after trauma is not about forcing yourself to 'feel' or 'think' differently. It’s about gently re-engaging with your body’s intelligence and nervous system regulation. Here are key approaches grounded in research:

  • Mindful Body Awareness: Deb Dana’s work on Polyvagal Theory suggests starting with noticing sensations without judgment—breath rhythm, abdominal expansion, or pelvic grounding (Dana, 2018).
  • Movement and Breath: Simple movements that mobilize the core, like pelvic tilts or diaphragmatic breathing, can stimulate vagal tone and foster safety (Levine, 1997; Porges, 2017).
  • Sensorimotor Psychotherapy: Pat Ogden’s approach integrates talk therapy with attention to bodily sensations, helping clients release muscular tension patterns in the core (Ogden et al., 2006).
  • Internal Family Systems (IFS): Richard Schwartz’s model helps identify and harmonize internal parts that may avoid core sensations due to trauma, facilitating compassionate self-leadership (Schwartz, 1995).
  • EMDR for Somatic Processing: Francine Shapiro’s EMDR therapy, while cognitive, also engages somatic processing pathways, often releasing stuck physiological responses in the core (Shapiro, 2018).

Practical Steps to Find Your Core After Trauma

Here is a simple, evidence-based practice to start reconnecting with your core safely:

  1. Set a safe space: Sit or lie down comfortably where you won’t be disturbed.
  2. Begin with breath awareness: Place one hand on your abdomen. Breathe naturally and notice the movement under your hand.
  3. Scan the core: Slowly bring your attention to the area between your ribs and pelvis. Notice any sensations: tightness, warmth, coolness, emptiness.
  4. Label sensations: Use simple words: "tight," "soft," "numb," or "alive." This naming helps regulate the nervous system (Siegel, 2010).
  5. Engage in gentle movement: Try small pelvic tilts or rocking to invite movement in the core without forcing.
  6. Pause and rest: Allow your body to integrate any shifts.

Remember that patience and safety are essential. If intense emotions arise, grounding techniques or support from a trauma-informed professional can help.

The Role of the Nervous System in Core Reconnection

Trauma dysregulates the autonomic nervous system (ANS), leading to chronic states of hyperarousal or shutdown. Dan Siegel’s concept of "window of tolerance" describes the optimal zone where one can experience sensations without becoming overwhelmed (Siegel, 2010).

Reconnecting with the body’s center helps expand this window by re-engaging the parasympathetic nervous system via the vagus nerve. Activities that promote ventral vagal activation—like safe social engagement, breathwork, and gentle movement—are key to restoring core regulation (Porges, 2017).

When to Seek Support and Professional Help

While self-directed somatic work can be powerful, trauma’s imprint can be complex and deeply embedded. If reconnecting with your core triggers overwhelming sensations, dissociation, or emotional flooding, consider working with a licensed somatic therapist trained in trauma treatment. Experts like Pat Ogden, Deb Dana, and Francine Shapiro stress the importance of a skilled guide for navigating difficult trauma material safely.

Conclusion: The Body’s Center as a Gateway to Healing

Reclaiming your body’s center after trauma is not a quick fix but a gradual process of restoring connection to yourself. Grounded in the research of van der Kolk, Levine, Porges, and others, somatic healing invites you to listen to your body’s wisdom and gently rebuild regulation and resilience.

Your core is where your nervous system finds balance, where your emotions become manageable, and where your authentic self can emerge. With patience, awareness, and the right tools, you can find your way back to this essential center.

faqs.q: How does trauma affect the body’s core?
faqs.a: Trauma often causes muscular tension, pain, or numbness in the core area and disrupts nervous system regulation, leading to disconnection from bodily sensations.

faqs.q: What is the role of the vagus nerve in trauma recovery?
faqs.a: The vagus nerve helps regulate the autonomic nervous system. Stimulating it through breath and movement promotes a sense of safety and reconnects the body’s center.

faqs.q: Can somatic practices alone heal trauma?
faqs.a: Somatic practices are powerful for nervous system regulation but may need to be combined with professional support, especially for complex trauma.

faqs.q: How can I safely start reconnecting with my core after trauma?
faqs.a: Begin with gentle breath awareness and mindful attention to core sensations, moving slowly and pausing whenever you feel overwhelmed.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book that explains how trauma is stored in the body and offers insights into somatic healing.

affiliateProducts.title: Waking the Tiger by Peter Levine
affiliateProducts.description: Introduces Somatic Experiencing and practical tools for releasing trauma held in the body’s core.

affiliateProducts.title: Polyvagal Exercises for Safety and Connection by Deb Dana
affiliateProducts.description: Provides accessible exercises grounded in Polyvagal Theory to help regulate the nervous system and reconnect with your body.

metaTitle: Trauma and the Body's Center: Find Your Core
metaDescription: Learn how trauma impacts your body's core and discover science-backed somatic methods to reconnect and heal effectively.
author: The Oracle Lover
